title: Effects of systemic glutamate on craniofacial pain
research_question: Does systemic administration of monosodium glutamate (MSG) affect craniofacial pain sensitivity in healthy individuals and myofascial TMD patients?
methods_summary: Randomized, double-blinded, placebo-controlled trials (cross-over and parallel-group designs)
Oral administration of MSG (150 mg/kg) or placebo (NaCl) in sugar-free lemon soda
Intramuscular microdialysis to measure interstitial glutamate in masseter muscle
Pressure pain threshold (PPT) and pressure pain tolerance (PPTol) measurements using algometer
Autonomic parameter monitoring (blood pressure, heart rate)
Food diary analysis for dietary glutamate intake
Local intramuscular glutamate injections to induce experimental pain (Study III)
dataset: 74 healthy participants (Studies I-III) and 12 myofascial TMD patients (Study IV); total N=86
key_findings: Repeated MSG intake (150 mg/kg/day for 5 days) increased interstitial glutamate in masseter muscle and plasma glutamate levels
MSG intake was associated with increased headache and pericranial muscle tenderness in healthy individuals
MSG did not significantly alter pressure pain thresholds or tolerance in healthy subjects or TMD patients
Acute MSG administration did not exacerbate ongoing pain in myofascial TMD patients
No significant correlation between interstitial, saliva, and plasma glutamate increase rates
MSG consumption did not affect autonomic parameters (blood pressure, heart rate)
extracted_insights: Systemic MSG intake elevates interstitial glutamate in jaw muscles and can induce headache and muscle tenderness in healthy individuals, but does not significantly alter mechanical pain sensitivity or exacerbate clinical pain in myofascial TMD patients. The findings suggest peripheral NMDA receptor mechanisms may mediate MSG effects, but tolerance may develop with repeated exposure.
